title: NIS2 Cryptography and Encryption Policy Monitoring
exclusions: Does not approve new PKI hierarchies, perform manual certificate rotations, waive regulatory mandates, or assess nation-state crypto regulations beyond organizational policy mapping.
evidence: Saved search outputs `UC-22.2.11` and `UC-22.2.11-stream`, supporting `stream:tls` samples for at least one incident review quarter, and TSA-sealed CSV archives per `evidenceSigning` retained ≥7 years.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(h) requires policies regarding the use of cryptography and encryption. The UC monitors TLS certificate posture, cipher suite compliance, and encryption policy deviations from Splunk telemetry but cannot evaluate whether the entity's cryptography policy adequately addresses key management lifecycle or regulatory minimums, hence partial.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(h) (Cryptography and encryption) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.11: NIS2 Cryptography and Encryption Policy Monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.11' running on CIM Certificate data model, index=network (TLS metadata), index=vulnerability (crypto-related findings), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Lab forwards a synthesized `Certificates` summary whose `ssl_end_time` is ten days out for subject `wallet-api.local` while Stream shows `tls_version=TLSv1.0`; combined dashboard lights CRITICAL on renewal and weak protocol within the same five-minute test window.
controlTest.negativeScenario: All in-scope subjects carry `notAfter` beyond forty-five days, Stream reports nothing below TLSv1.2, and `nis2_crypto_exception_register.csv` contains a valid waiver row for the lone legacy mainframe listener; nightly correlation search returns zero breaches after filters apply.

dataSources: Accelerated Splunk CIM Certificates data model (`datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates`, fields ssl_subject_common_name, ssl_issuer_common_name, ssl_end_time); `index=pki` `sourcetype=stream:tls` from Splunk Add-on for Stream ([Splunkbase 1809](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1809)); `index=certificates` `sourcetype=certificate:inventory` for CMDB scans; optional Tenable NVS feed `index=vulnerability`.
app: Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621)
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates
  where All_Certificates.ssl_end_time < relative_time(now(), "+30d")
  by All_Certificates.ssl_subject_common_name All_Certificates.ssl_end_time All_Certificates.ssl_issuer_common_name
| rename All_Certificates.* as *
| eval days_until_expiry=round((ssl_end_time - now())/86400, 0)
| eval status=case(days_until_expiry < 0, "EXPIRED", days_until_expiry < 14, "CRITICAL", days_until_expiry < 30, "WARNING")
| sort days_until_expiry
| table ssl_subject_common_name, ssl_issuer_common_name, days_until_expiry, status
description: Surfaces X.509 certificates whose `ssl_end_time` falls inside a thirty-day horizon using accelerated CIM Certificate summaries so crypto governance teams can renew before services break or downgrade ciphers.
value: Expired orsoon-to-expire keys silently undermine encrypted channels that regulators expect to stay trustworthy; anchoring renewals to Splunk schedules gives measurable lead time, owner accountability, and historical proof that the organization actively managed cryptography—not just bought it once.
implementation: (1) Install CIM ([Splunkbase 1621](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1621)) and accelerate **Certificates**. (2) Forward Stream `stream:tls` to `index=pki`. (3) Import inventory scanner output as `certificate:inventory`. (4) Map Tenable plugin families about weak crypto to `index=vulnerability`. (5) Maintain `nis2_crypto_exception_register.csv` with compensating controls. (6) Email PKI ops when `status=CRITICAL`.
visualization: Table of expiring subjects, pie chart of issuer share, overlay of renewal tickets from ServiceNow.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t latest(Certificates.x509_ssl_hash) from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates by Certificates.ssl_subject | head 10000
knownFalsePositives: 1) **Short-lived Let's Encrypt auto-renewals** tripping WARN every sixty days. *Distinguish:* issuer `ssl_issuer_common_name` contains "Let's Encrypt". *Suppress:* `where not match(ssl_issuer_common_name,"(?i)let")`. 2) **Tenable reporting duplicate certs per IP** when SNI not captured. *Distinguish:* same `ssl_subject_common_name` with different `dest_ip`. *Suppress:* `| stats min(days_until_expiry) as days_until_expiry by ssl_subject_common_name`. 3) **Stream sampling misses occasional handshakes** so `status` flips stale. *Distinguish:* `index=certificates` inventory shows newer `ssl_end_time`. *Suppress:* prefer inventory lookup when `_time` within 24h. 4) **Internal CA names flagged weak by generic regex** because of legacy templates still permitted under national banking guidance. *Distinguish:* serial listed in `nis2_crypto_exception_register.csv`. *Suppress:* `lookup nis2_crypto_exception_register ssl_subject OUTPUT waive | where isnull(waive)`. 5) **Dev/test clusters inheriting wildcard prod certs** creating noise. *Distinguish:* host suffix `.dev.`. *Suppress:* filter `where NOT match(ssl_subject_common_name,"(?i)\\*.dev")`.
equipmentModels: Splunk Common Information Model Add-on 5.x (Certificates data model)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (asset framework, notable routing)
Splunk Stream Independent Forwarder (wire data TLS metadata capture)
Tenable.io / Tenable.sc (vulnerability and certificate scan data)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Data Model Acceleration**: The CIM datamodel referenced in the search must be accelerated. Validate with `| rest /services/admin/summarization | search summary.id=*` and confirm `summary.complete=100`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(h)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(h) (Cryptography and encryption) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.11: NIS2 Cryptography and Encryption Policy Monitoring." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


6 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Install CIM ([Splunkbase 1621](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1621)) and accelerate **Certificates**. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 2:** Forward Stream `stream:tls` to `index=pki`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Import inventory scanner output as `certificate:inventory`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Map Tenable plugin families about weak crypto to `index=vulnerability`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** Maintain `nis2_crypto_exception_register.csv` with compensating controls. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 6:** Email PKI ops when `status=CRITICAL`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Cryptography and Encryption Policy Monitoring," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the NIS2 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search uses `| tstats` against a CIM-accelerated data model rather than reading raw events. The performance delta is typically 10-50x over raw search, and for large indexes can be >100x. The acceleration summaries are maintained on a rolling window (default: 1 year, configurable in the data model editor) and are queried from indexer-local summary files rather than re-scanning raw events.

CIM acceleration dependency: this search FAILS SILENTLY if the underlying data model is not accelerated. It will return zero rows instead of raising an error, which is exactly the wrong failure mode for a compliance detection. Verify acceleration status before scheduling:

```spl
| rest /services/admin/summarization
| search title="<datamodel_name>"
| table title access_count summary.complete summary.earliest_time summary.latest_time
```

`summary.complete` should show 100 (or very close). If it's below 95, the acceleration is still catching up or has stalled — check the DMA audit log (`index=_audit action=dma_run`) for errors.

Aggregation choice: `count()` is grouped by `All_Certificates.ssl_subject_common_name, All_Certificates.ssl_end_time, All_Certificates.ssl_issuer_common_name`. This produces a summary row per distinct combination, which is typically what compliance reporting requires (one row per asset, one row per user, one row per rule). If downstream processing expects a flat event stream, avoid `tstats` — summary rows are not raw events and cannot be drilled into without re-running against the raw index.

When NOT to use tstats: if the field being filtered was added after the acceleration started, or is search-time extracted by an app the acceleration isn't aware of, the field will not exist in the summaries and the `where` clause will drop every row. Rebuild the acceleration after any field-extraction changes.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Certificates` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t latest(Certificates.x509_ssl_hash) from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates by Certificates.ssl_subject | head 10000
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Certificates` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.11: NIS2 Cryptography and Encryption Policy Monitoring]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.11,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `days_until_expiry`, `ssl_issuer_common_name`, `ssl_subject_common_name`, `status`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table days_until_expiry, ssl_issuer_common_name, ssl_subject_common_name, status
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `days_until_expiry`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(days_until_expiry) as p50 perc90(days_until_expiry) as p90 perc99(days_until_expiry) as p99 max(days_until_expiry) as hi
```

The current threshold `0` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.11: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Expiring Subjects (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Pie Chart Of Issuer Share (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Overlay Of Renewal Tickets From Servicenow (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Zero results when acceleration is fresh:** The field you're filtering on was likely added after the acceleration started — rebuild the acceleration.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We keep an eye on the electronic locks (certificates) that make websites and internal tools talk privately. When a lock is about to expire or is too old-fashioned, we get a heads-up so nothing suddenly stops working.
